questions.text: Answer part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) "The story of the European Parliament is one of gradual transformation from a relatively powerless Assembly under the 1952 ECSC Treaty to the considerably strengthened institution it represents today" (Craig & De Burca, EU Law: Texts, Cases and Materials,(Oxford, OUP, 4^th^ Edition),2007, p .58). By reference, inter alia, to Treaty provisions and relevant case-law of the Court of Justice analyse this statement with particular reference to: (i) the evolution of the European Parliament role in the EU legislative process; (ii) The features that distinguish its legislative role from that of any national parliament. [Or]{.underline} (B) "Member State maintain the right to assess whether or not, in issuing particular legal provisions the [Union's] legislative organs acted within [their conferred powers] and in accordance with the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality. Should the adoption of provisions infringe these and frameworks, the principle of precedence of [Union] law fails to apply to such provisions"(see the Polish Constitutional Court, Judgement K18/04 of 11 May 2005 re. Polish Membership of the EU). Analyse critically by reference to the case-law of the Court of Justice regarding the principles of primacy (precedence) and autonomy of Union law, as well as the principles of conferred powers, subsidiarity and proportionality, which are now codified in Article 5 TEU, whether the view expressed by the Polish Constitutional Court is compatible with the said principles of primacy and autonomy of Union law and the principle of conferred powers or competences, as interpreted by the Court of Justice. Having regard to the latter's case-law, does it fall to the national constitutional courts, in the final instance, to review whether Union acts comply with the principles of conferred powers, subsidiarity and proportionality?

questions.description: "The most significant deepening of the integration process by the Lisbon Treaty occurred in the fields of criminal law…
questions.text: "The most significant deepening of the integration process by the Lisbon Treaty occurred in the fields of criminal law and policing" (Chalmers, Davies & Monti, European Union Law, 2^nd^ end. (2010), p. 583). Discuss this option with particular reference to the provisions of Title V of part Three of the TFEU and relevant case-law of the Court of Justice on Title V and on its predecessor, the former TEU' Third Pillar' on justice and home affairs.

Answer Parts (A) [and]{.underline} (B). (A) Analyse by reference to relevant case-law the interpretation by the Union judicature of the standing requirements of "direct and individual concern" under the fourth paragraph of what is now Article 263 TFEU (formerly Article 230 EC) for natural or legal persons. Are these requirements interpreted too strictly? [And]{.underline} (B) Consider and assess the significance of the amendment to Article 263 TFEU introduced by the Treaty of Lisbon pertaining to "regulatory" assets "do not entail implementing measures."

questions.description: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following eight judgments, setting out their core facts, the key issues…
questions.text: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following eight judgments, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice and/or General Court: (i) Case 22/70 Commission v Council ('ERTA') [1971] ECR 263; (ii) Case T-342/99 Airtours v Commission [2002] ECR II-2585 (iii) Case C-112/00 Schmidberger v Austria [2003] ECR I-5659; (iv) Case C-397/01 to C-403/01 Pfeiffer and Others [2004] ECR I-8835; (v) Case T-201/04 Microsoft v Commission [2007] ECCR II-3601; (vi) Joined Cases C-402/06 P and C-415/05 P Kadi and Al Barakaat International Foundation v. Council [2008] ECR I-6351; (vii) C-236/09 Association belge des Consommateurs Test-Achats and Others v. Council [2011] ECR 1-nyr (Judgment of 01/03/2011); (viii) Case C-34/09 Zambrano [2011] ECR 1-nyr (Judgment of 08/03/2011).

questions.description: Hristo, an engineer, is a Bulgarian national and resident who was subject… Company.
questions.text: Hristo, an engineer, is a Bulgarian national and resident who was subject to an order prohibiting him from leaving Bulgarian territory. The order was adopted by the Minister for the interior in 2008 on the basis of a 1999 Bulgarian law and prevented him from being issued with a passport or replacement identity documents. The Bulgarian law permits the adoption of such orders restricting the right of free movement in circumstances where "persons owe considerable debts", defined as in excess of the euro equivalent of 5,000 Bulgarian Lev (BGN), or approximately €2,500, "established by court order, to Bulgarian or foreign natural or legal persons, unless their personal assets cover the debt or they provide appropriate security". The 2008 order was adopted at the request of a bailiff on account of a debt owed to a financial institution of the euro equivalent of some BGN 200,000. Histro did not challenge the order before the Bulgarian courts at the time and it became final. FE-1 Ltd is an engineering company anxious to expand its business into the Bulgarian market and wishes to engage Hristo as a consultant. Hristo's work will necessarily involve him in travelling back and forth to Ireland. FE-1 Ltd has asked you as its solicitor to advise it as to whether: (i) the prohibition on Hristo's leaving Bulgaria may be challenged by reference to EU law; [and]{.underline} (ii) A Bulgarian provision which provides that any person affected by a decision taken in implementation of a provision of national law which is determined to be incompatible with EU law, may contest its application ex nunc (for the future) only, and provided that an administrative court (judicial review type) application is taken by that person against such decision within 30 days of the incompatibility with EU law of the underlying Bulgarian provision being established. Where a provision of Bulgarian law is found to be incompatible with the Bulgarian constitution, the time limit for challenging any decisions taking in implementation of that provision is 45 days. Set out your advice to FE-I Ltd. Having regard to relevant Treaty provisions, general principles, secondary EU legislation and case-law.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of [any three]{.underline} of the following four (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices…
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of [any three]{.underline} of the following four (hypothetical) Irish measures and practices with Union law, particularly the citizenship rules and, as the case may be, Articles 28, 30, 34, 36, 45, 49, 56 and 110 TFEU: (i) A Levy on the exportation of electricity generated from wind turbines located within the State and that are connected to the national grid, which is levied on the wind turbine operators, the proceeds of which are paid into a special fund maintained by the department of the Environment. Payments are made out of this fund by way of grants to persons living within a kilometre of such a wind turbine(s) for remedial measures taken by such persons to their houses to reduce the effects on such persons and their families of noise and blade flicker from such turbines. Grants are also paid to undertakings developing wind farms to alleviate the cost to them of procuring and establishing a connection to the national grid. (ii) A decision of the Minister for Justice and Equality to deport a Mr. I, an Italian national, back to Italy on foot of a recommendation from the Court of Criminal Appeal that such deportation be considered when I. Completes a term of imprisonment of seven years for the sexual assault, sexual coercion and rape of a minor, of which I. Was convicted in 2010. The Minister considers that because of the recommendation of the Court he is entitled to deport I. And so has not sought representations on I.s behalf. The acts which gave rise to that conviction took place between 1999 and 2009. From 2002, I. compelled his victim to have sexual intercourse with him or perform other sexual acts on an almost weekly basis by using force and threatening to kill her mother or brother. The victim of the criminal offences was his former partner's daughter, who was 8 years old when the offences commenced. Mr. I came to I relend in 1993 and worked in the restaurant trade before establishing his own fast-food outlet in 1997. I.'s only sibling and parents came from Italy in 1998 to work in the business he had established and have resided and worked in Ireland ever since. With the result that virtually all of I.' Family links are now with Irish rather than Italian society. I. Will be 60 years of age when he completes his sentence and has developed a number of significant medical problems, including hypertension, in recent years. (iii) An employment promotion measure which makes the grant to employers of a subsidy for the recruitment of unemployed persons aged over 45 years subject to the condition that the unemployed person recruited has been registered as a job seeker in the State with the Department of Social Protection, in circumstances where obtaining such registration is subject, in practice, to a condition of residence within the State for a period of usually at least six months prior to registration. (iv) A Copyright Amendment Act that prohibits the use of satellite decoders purchased or acquired outside of the State by, or on behalf of, undertakings active in the hospitality industry, including public houses, hotels and restaurants, in order to screen showings of live sporting events taking place outside the State.

Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) By reference to the Court of Justice's case-law interpreting Article 63(1) TFEU, discuss what constitutes a capital movement for the purpose of the Treaty, what types of national measures are likely to be regarded as prohibited restrictions thereon and what grounds of justification may be advanced by Member States for such restrictions. [OR]{.underline} (B) Consider whether Council Regulation (EC) No. 44/2001 on Jurisdiction and the Enforcement of Judgments in Civil and Commercial Matters ("the Brussels I Regulation") establishes clear jurisdictional rules for determining which national courts have jurisdiction in civil and commercial matters involving inter-state elements. In your answer refer, in particular, to the scope and operation of the principal jurisdiction rule under Article 2(1) of Regulation No. 44/2001 and the special rules prescribed in Article 5(1) and (3) of the Regulation, and to relevant case-law of the Court of Justice.

Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. (A) With reference to relevant case-law and Commission decisions, analyse critically the extent to which Article 106 TFEU provides for an exception to the application of EU competition law, particularly Articles 101 and 102 TFEU, and the scope and limits to any such exception as regards public undertakings, undertakings to which Member States have granted special or exclusive rights and undertakings entrusted by Member States with the operation of services of general economic interest. [OR]{.underline} (B) Ireland exempted from excise duty fossil fuels used for the production of alumina in the Shannon region between 1983 and 2005. In 2006, the exemption was replaced with a reduced rate. Neither measure was notified to the Commission as potential State aids pursuant to Article 108(3) TFEU. The operator of an alumina factory near Enniskillen has complained to the Commission about what it regards as the State aid comprised in both measures, on foot of which the Commission initiated in 2007 the procedure provided for in Article 108(2) TFEU with regard both to the earlier exemption and the new reduced excise duty rate. In a resulting decision adopted in 2009, the Commission decided that both the exemption and reduced rate are States aids that are incompatible with the internal market. However, the Commission decision only orders Ireland to recover any difference between the reduced rate and the normal rate of excise duty from the date of introduction of the reduced rate in 2005. Prepare an opinion advising the Attorney General on behalf of the Irish Government on: (i) whether there are grounds to challenge the Commission decision. Before the EU judicature, explaining to the procedure to be followed, including time limits, for such a challenge and before which EU court it courts it should be brought; [And]{.underline} (ii) whether the limitation on the obligation to recover the aid established in the decision could be challenged by the complainant, and if so what procedure would it have to follow.

questions.description: Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B) Do not attempt both parts. Proportionality, Directive.
questions.text: Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B) Do not attempt both parts. (A) "The limits of Union competences are governed by the principle of conferral. The use of Union competences is governed by the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality "(Article 5(1) TEU). By reference particularly to Treaty provisions and relevant case-law of the Court of Justice, analyse critically: (i) the importance and day-to-day significance of the principles of conferral, subsidiarity and proportionality in the exercise by the Union's political institutions of their powers under the Treaties; (ii) the principles, including the ones mentioned above, which offer the greatest protection to Member States and regional governments in the Union against over encroachment by the Union on their prerogatives and powers. [Or]{.underline} (B) By reference particularly to Treaty provisions and relevant case-law of the Court of Justice compare and contrast: <!-- --> (i) the legal nature of a "regulation", a "directive" and a "decision" in Union law; (ii) the core differences between a "legislative act" and a "non-legislative act" in Union law as regards both their nature and the means by which they are adopted; (iii) the difference between "delegated'' acts and "implementing" acts in Union law?

questions.description: "The most significant deepening of the integration process by the Lisbon Treaty occurred in the fields of criminal law…
questions.text: "The most significant deepening of the integration process by the Lisbon Treaty occurred in the fields of criminal law and policing" (Chalmers, Davies & Monti, European Union Law, 2nd ed. (2010), p. 583). Discuss this opinion with particular reference to the provisions of Title V of Part Three of the TFEU on the 'Area of Freedom, Justice and Security' and relevant case-law of the Court of Justice on Title V and on its predecessor, the former TEU 'Third Pillar' on justice and home affairs.

questions.description: The infringement action under Articles 258 to 260 TFEU plays an essential role in guaranteeing the correct application…
questions.text: The infringement action under Articles 258 to 260 TFEU plays an essential role in guaranteeing the correct application of Union law and "is the ultima rato enabling Union interests enshrined in the Treaties to prevail over inertial and resistance of Member States" (Court of Justice in Case 20/59 Italy v. High Authority [1960] ECR 325, 339). Discuss this view critically by reference to the procedures involved in actions brought under Articles 258 TFEU, 259 TFEU and 260(2) TFEU, as interpreted in relevant case-law. Consider also whether the infringement action, as it has been amended in particular by the Treaty of Maastricht giving the power for the Court of Justice in certain circumstances to impose financial sanctions, performs effectively the radical role for which it was envisaged, namely the autonomous enforcement of Treaty obligations by an independent supranational court.

questions.description: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following eight judgments, setting out their core facts, the key issues…
questions.text: Write a note on [any three]{.underline} of the following eight judgments, setting out their core facts, the key issues of EU law that arose for consideration and how those issues were addressed by the Court of Justice and/or General Court: (i) Case 22/70 Commission v Council ('ERTA') [1971] ECR 263; (ii) Case C-91/92 Faccini Dori [1994] ECR I-1337; (iii) Case T-342/99 Airtours v Commission [2002] ECR II-2585; (iv) Case C-224/01 Köbler [2003] ECR I-10239; (v) Case T-342/07 Ryanair Holdings v Commission [2010] ECR II-3457; (vi) C-12/11 McDonagh v Ryanair [2013] ECR I-nyr (Judgment of 31 January 2013); (vii) Case C-364110 Hungary v Slovak Republic [2012] ECR 1-nyr (Judgment of 16/12/2012); (viii) Case C-34/09 Pringle v Government of Ireland, Ireland and The Attorney General [2012] ECR 1-nyr (Judgment of 27/11/2012).

questions.description: FE-1 is an environmental protection group formed by a group of concerned… Constitutional, Directive.
questions.text: FE-1 is an environmental protection group formed by a group of concerned law students. FE-1 has sought access to various documents held by the Department of the Environment, Community and Local Government regarding Ireland's greenhouse gas emissions, including a report of a discussion at Cabinet of the issue, pursuant to Directive 2003/4/EC of the European Parliament and the Council of 28 January 2003 on Public Access to Environmental Information. The Department granted access to all the documents to which access was sought, save for the report based on Cabinet confidentiality and because the Irish regulations transposing the Directive into Irish law exclude "Cabinet records". The Directive, which provides for access to environmental information, contains no such exclusion. FE-1 considers that the Department's refusal is incompatible with the Directive and has sought your advice as to whether it could challenge the refusal based on EU law and, if so, whether it could bring an administrative appeal to the Commissioner for Environmental Information seeking access to the document by asking the Commissioner directly to apply the Directive, or whether it must first challenge before the High Court the compatibility with the Directive of the Irish regulations implementing it. Consider also whether the Directive takes precedence over the Irish constitutional doctrine of 'Cabinet confidentiality'. Set out your advice to FE-1 by reference to relevant EU law Treaty provisions, general principles of Union law and case-law.

questions.description: Assess the compatibility of [any two]{.underline} of the following four… Directive, Shareholder.
questions.text: Assess the compatibility of [any two]{.underline} of the following four (hypothetical) measures and practices with Union law, particularly (as relevant) with Articles 18 and 20-21, 34, 36, 45, 49, 56 and 63 TFEU, as well as Directive 2004/38 on the right of citizens and their families to move and reside freely within the territory of the Member States, and provide the required legal advices to the clients concerned: (i) A national law that requires all bread bakeries, or those engaged in commercial bread baking activities, to have an operating licence, and which subjects the grant of such a licence to compliance with a number of physical requirements, such as having a specified area for kneading bread and having a flour stone. Under this law, 'FE-1 Value', a supermarket, that has no bread-baking licence, has been prosecuted for producing bread by baking bought-in frozen dough at the bakery counter of its various retail premises. Advise FE-1Value on whether it could have a defence based on EU law to the prosecution, referring to relevant Treaty provisions and case-law. (ii) A national law that provides that no shareholder in Aer Lingus may have more than 20 per cent of the voting rights regardless of the actual size of its shareholding, that a variety of important strategic decisions for the airline, including whether to sell landing slots or open new routes, can be blocked by a blocking minority of 20 per cent of shareholders and that the Minister for Transport shall hold and retain a 20 per cent shareholding. The Government has received a letter of formal notice from the Commission under Article 258 TFEU stating the European Commission's opinion that this law is incompatible with the EU's free movement of capital rules and the Attorney General has asked you to prepare a draft response to the Commission's letter refuting the allegation. Prepare the draft response by reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case­law; (iii) A decision of the Irish Minister for Justice to deport Stefano, an Italian national, back to Italy on foot of a recommendation from the Court of Criminal Appeal that such deportation be considered when Stefano completes a term of imprisonment of seven years for the sexual assault, sexual coercion and rape of a minor, of which he was convicted in 2010. The Minister considers that because of the recommendation of the Court, he is entitled to deport Stefano, and so has sought no representations on Stefano's behalf. The sexual acts which gave rise to that conviction took place between 1999 and 2009. The victim of the criminal offences was his former partner's daughter, who was 8 years old when the offences commenced. Stefano came to Ireland in 1993 and worked in the restaurant trade before establishing his own fast-food outlet in 1997. Stefano's only sibling and parents came from Italy in 1998 to work in the business he had established and have resided and worked in Ireland ever since, with the result that virtually all his family links are now with Irish rather than Italian society. Stefano will be 60 years of age when he completes his sentence and has developed in recent years a number of significant medical problems, including hypertension. Advise Stefano, by reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case-law, as to whether he could challenge the deportation decision by reference to EU law; (iv) An Irish employment-promotion law that provides for a system of vouchers, which unemployed persons in receipt of jobseekers' allowance are given to provide to private recruitment agencies. The latter may redeem them for a minimum payment of €1,000 from the Department of Jobs, Enterprise and Innovation (''DJEI ') if they find the unemployed person a job that removes him/her from the live register for a period of at least three months, and provided no fee is charged by the agency to the jobseeker. The payment out on a voucher rises to €5,000 if a permanent job is found for the jobseeker. However, the law also provides that the placement must be with an employer registered in the State. Flora, an unemployed solicitor, registered with 'FE-1 Jobfind', and signed a contract specifying that she would have to pay it an agency fee for finding her a job if the DJEI did not redeem the voucher she gave it. FE-1 Jobfind found Flora a one year position with a law firm in Newry, Northern Ireland, but the DJEI has refused to redeem the voucher as the firm is not subject to making compulsory social security contributions in the State. Fearful that she will now be pursued by the FE-1 Jobfind for its agency fee, Flora has sought your advice as to whether the requirement that the employer found must be registered and subject to social security contribution in the State is compatible with the freedom of movement provisions of EU law, and, if so, whether she may rely on those provisions as against FE-1 Jobfind, or would have to join the Stare to any action brought against her by FE-1 Jobfind seeking payment of its agency fee. Set out your advice by reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case-law.

questions.description: Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B). Breach Of Contract, Damages.
questions.text: Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B). Do [not]{.underline} attempt both parts. (A) "Of all social matters addressed by Union law it is the area of sex discrimination and pay that has deepest roots in the Treaties and keenest teeth" (see 'Edward and Lane on European Union Law', 2013, p. 867). By reference to relevant Treaty provisions, directives and case-law, assess the effectiveness of the teeth that Union law has developed in its prohibition of discrimination based on sex both, within and without the workplace, in the EU. Assess in particular the contribution of the case-law of the Court of Justice to this development and consider whether you would agree that it represents one of the Union's finest social achievements to date. [Or]{.underline} (B) Tom, an Irish citizen and domiciled in Ireland, had an accident whilst swimming in a swimming pool at an acquaintance, Mary's, holiday home in Turkey. Tom had rented the holiday home from Mary, who is also an Irish citizen who resides for part of the year in Ireland and part of the year in Turkey. All of the witnesses to the accident reside in Turkey. Tom has brought an action for damages for personal injuries against Mary for breach of contract and/or breach of duty in the High Court in Ireland. Mary has sought your advice as to whether there are grounds, consistent with Council Regulation (EC) No 44/2001 of 22 December 2000 on jurisdiction and the recognition and enforcement of judgments in civil and commercial matters (the 'Brussels 1 Regulation'), upon which the jurisdiction of the Irish court to hear and determine Tom's claim could be resisted. She has informed you that shortly before she was served with Tom's summons, she instituted proceedings in Turkey seeking a declaration from a Turkish court that a third party domiciled in Turkey was responsible for Tom's accident. Set out your advice to Mary with reference to the relevant provisions of the Brussels 1 Regulation and any relevant case-law interpreting those provisions.

Answer Part (A) [or]{.underline} Part (B). Do not attempt both parts. (A) FE-1 Ferries Ltd. operates passenger ferries from Rosaveel, Co. Galway, to the Arran Islands. The port is also used by various fishing trawlers and the harbour facilities have been extensively refurbished to attract more national and other EU-registered trawlers to land their catches at Rosaveel. The port is owned by the Minister for Agriculture and Fisheries. In order to expedite, because of the recession, the repayment of the loan drawn down by the State to finance the extensive port upgrade, the Oireachtas passed emergency legislation allowing the Minister to increase harbour dues and fees and expressly permitted to impose higher increases on non-fishery users of the port. Thereunder, the Minister adopted an order increasing the average fees paid by non-fishery users of the port by 20%, whilst those imposed on fishery users were on average only increased by 10%. FE-1 Ferries is the largest non-fishery user of the port and the effect of the increases has been to threaten the viability of the passenger services it operates. There is no other suitable port that it can use on the western seaboard of the State from which to operate its services to the Arran Islands. It has sought your advice as to whether it could challenge by reference to EU competition law the differentiated increases in the fees it has been required to pay by the Minister to the Minister qua port owner. Set out your advice by reference to relevant Treaty provisions and case-law. [Or]{.underline} (B) Assess the powers of the Commission to carry out inspections ('dawn raids') under Article 20 of Council Regulation No 1/2003 on the implementation of the rules on competition laid down in Articles 81 and 82 of the Treaty [now Articles 101 and 102 TFEU]. What limits on the power and protections for those subject to its exercise do, in particular, the general principles of Union law, as interpreted in relevant case-law of the Court of Justice, impose?
